mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Friday, April 3rd.

Starting with Breeze. No new Breeze coverage in the last 24 hours.

Now for industry and competitor news.

Bloomberg reported on April 2nd that the U.S.-Iran war has driven global jet fuel to nearly 195 dollars per barrel — more than double the pre-conflict level of around 85 dollars — as the Strait of Hormuz closure has cut tanker shipping by more than 70 percent, with United Airlines already announcing a 5 percent cut to its Q2 and Q3 schedule and American Airlines C.E.O. Robert Isom warning of a 400 million dollar Q1 fuel hit. Breeze's all-domestic route network shields the carrier from the Middle East airspace closures grounding Gulf and European carriers, but jet fuel costs have risen 88 percent in North America and leadership should determine whether current leisure fare levels still provide adequate margin or whether a targeted fuel surcharge is now appropriate.

The International Business Times reported on April 3rd that Kuwait International Airport remains closed following a series of Iranian drone strikes that set fuel storage tanks ablaze near the terminal, leaving Kuwait Airways operating contingency flights from neighboring Saudi and Bahraini airports with no official reopening timeline announced. Breeze has no direct operational exposure to Middle East airspace disruptions, but the same Strait of Hormuz blockade driving this regional chaos is the supply-side mechanism behind the jet fuel cost spike that now threatens 2026 margins for every domestic U.S. carrier including Breeze.

C.N.B.C. reported on April 2nd that the Senate passed a D.H.S. funding bill and President Trump signed an executive order to restore T.S.A. pay — a direct response to more than 500 agents quitting and absenteeism rates exceeding 40 percent at some checkpoints — but the House has not yet acted, and the partial government shutdown enters Easter weekend still unresolved. For Breeze communications, this is a live passenger-experience risk: with T.S.A. lines running hours long at major airports and travelers already primed for frustration during the busiest weekend of the spring, the team should monitor all Breeze market airports and be ready to communicate proactively if checkpoint delays affect boarding times.

Atlanta News First reported on April 1st that Hartsfield-Jackson Airport is on track to break its single-day T.S.A. screening record on April 4th with an estimated 115,000 security screenings, and Airlines for America forecasts a record 171 million passengers will fly in March and April combined — the strongest spring travel period the industry has ever recorded. Breeze's leisure-focused route network and aggressive fare pricing should position the carrier to capture a meaningful share of this Easter surge — revenue management should pull current load factor data across Breeze markets to confirm whether performance is tracking ahead of last year.

Community Impact reported on April 2nd that the Fort Worth City Council will vote April 28th on D.F.W. Airport's request to authorize up to 3 billion dollars in bond issuances this year, the opening phase of an 11 billion dollar capital improvement program spanning a new Terminal F, major Terminal C renovation, and International Parkway reconstruction. American Airlines' extraordinary long-term bet on Texas hub capacity should prompt Breeze's network planning team to assess whether growing D.F.W. volumes will funnel more Texas leisure demand that Breeze's San Antonio routes can capture — or whether a dominant expanded A.A. hub creates pricing pressure Breeze needs to plan around.

N.P.R. and Axios both published retrospective analyses on April 2nd marking the one-year anniversary of President Trump's Liberation Day tariffs, noting the tariffs were struck down by the Supreme Court in February and left behind higher consumer prices, a decline of 89,000 manufacturing jobs, and ongoing global supply-chain disruption. For Breeze, the resolved tariff uncertainty removes a risk that had clouded aircraft parts and fleet delivery costs, but the documented economic softness from the past year — particularly reduced consumer purchasing power — is worth incorporating into any near-term revenue forecasting assumptions for leisure travel demand.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
